scientificName: Synodontis petricola — CAUTION: virtually all fish sold under this name in the aquarium trade are actually S. lucipinnis (Dwarf Petricola, fw-fish-327), a smaller species formally described in 2006. True S. petricola reaches ~14 cm and is rarely exported; confirm ID before purchase.
alsoKnownAs: Cuckoo Catfish (shared trade name — also misapplied to S. multipunctatus)

nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral and sub-littoral zones of Lake Tanganyika; shelters in crevices and caves among tumbled boulders and cobble at shallow to mid depths, typically 5–40 m

decorPreferences: numerous caves and crevices formed by stacked flat rocks or slate
smooth rounded boulders and cobble mimicking Tanganyikan rocky littoral
tight rock piles with interconnecting tunnels large enough for adult fish
sandy or fine-gravel substrate between rock structures
avoid sharp substrate edges that may abrade the fleshy barbels
minimal or no planting required; Java fern or Anubias on rock tolerated

diet.notes: In the wild, the Petricola Catfish grazes on aufwuchs — the thin layer of algae, biofilm, and microscopic invertebrates that coats rocky surfaces — and scavenges opportunistically for detritus and small prey. In captivity it accepts a wide range of sinking prepared and frozen foods, including high-quality catfish pellets, algae wafers, frozen bloodworm, frozen brine shrimp, mysis, and daphnia. Because this species is nocturnal, sinking foods should be offered after lights-out so it can feed before diurnal tankmates intercept them. Vegetable matter such as blanched courgette or spinach, and vitamin-enriched frozen foods, are recommended to support long-term health.

goodWith: Tanganyikan cichlids of robust size (Tropheus, Petrochromis, Cyphotilapia frontosa)
other Synodontis species of similar or larger size
shell-dwellers with adequate rock caves nearby
avoidWith: small fish or fry (opportunistic predation — larger than S. lucipinnis, greater risk)
fish requiring soft or acidic water (incompatible chemistry)
soft-water tetras, rasboras, or South American cichlids
very small shell-dweller cichlids that may be harassed or crowded out of caves

imageCandidates.notes: Mike Peel; 5184x3456. Sharp full lateral profile of a live, vibrant individual showing the diagnostic large black body spots and black-and-white banded fins with white leading edges; additional shoal members visible behind (shoaling species). Note: S. petricola is visually near-identical to S. lucipinnis.

FakirNL; 3812x2937. Two live individuals among tumbled rockwork mimicking the Lake Tanganyika rocky littoral biotope; shows spotting and banded caudal/dorsal fins. Slightly softer than the recommended pick but represents shoaling and habitat well.
de:user:Nerezza; 655x425. Crisp clean full lateral view over sand, clearly showing spot pattern and white-edged banded fins; lower resolution, kept as a supporting textbook-style shot.

summary: The Petricola Catfish (Synodontis petricola) is rarely the fish actually sold under that name — most aquarium specimens are the smaller Synodontis lucipinnis. True S. petricola reaches around 14 cm and requires hard, alkaline Tanganyikan water. As a scaleless catfish, it is fatally sensitive to copper-based medications and does best kept in groups of at least three.
careNotes:
SPECIES IDENTITY WARNING: Synodontis petricola is the original "Petricola Catfish", described in 1959, but it is now known that the vast majority of fish sold under this name are actually Synodontis lucipinnis — see [fw-fish-327]. S. lucipinnis was previously lumped with S. petricola before a formal taxonomic separation in 2006; it remains far more common in the trade because it is smaller, more prolific in export collections, and nearly identical in colour pattern to the eye. True S. petricola grows to approximately 14 cm, compared with around 9 cm for S. lucipinnis, and is only sporadically available from specialist Lake Tanganyika importers. If a fish labelled "petricola" is under 10 cm at apparent adult size, it is almost certainly S. lucipinnis.

Care requirements for true S. petricola are essentially identical to those of S. lucipinnis, but scaled up for a larger fish. A 200 L tank with a minimum 120 cm footprint is recommended to house a group of three adults with adequate territorial caves. Lake Tanganyika water chemistry is non-negotiable: pH 8.0–9.0, GH 10–20°H, KH 10–18°H, temperature 23–27°C. Soft or acidic water leads to chronic stress, colour loss, and a shortened lifespan.

As a scaleless catfish, S. petricola is highly sensitive to copper-based medications — no copper treatment should be used in a tank housing this species, even at low concentrations. Formalin-based or salt-based alternatives are recommended when treating disease.

The species is strictly nocturnal; sinking foods should be offered after lights-out to prevent cichlid tankmates from intercepting them first. Décor should emphasise multiple rock caves and crevices of sufficient size for adult fish (approximately 5–6 cm opening). Groups of at least three are recommended, as solitary specimens tend to become withdrawn and may refuse food. Strong oxygenation consistent with the well-oxygenated nature of Lake Tanganyika should be maintained.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has been reported but is uncommon and less well-documented than for S. lucipinnis. S. petricola is understood to be an egg-scatterer that deposits eggs among rock crevices; no parental care is given, and adults will consume eggs if not removed. In the wild, the species reportedly exploits mouthbrooding cichlids — depositing eggs near a spawning pair so that the fry are inadvertently taken up and incubated by the cichlid host, a behaviour known as cuckoo parasitism that is shared with the related Synodontis multipunctatus. Replicating this in captivity requires pairing S. petricola with a Tanganyikan mouthbrooder such as Tropheus. Fry require a separate rearing tank and should be started on infusoria or finely powdered fry food, transitioning to micro-pellets at around two to three weeks. Sexual maturity is generally reached at approximately 18 to 24 months. Captive spawning remains inconsistent and is considered difficult to achieve reliably.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Synodontis petricola
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to ~14 cm), temperature 23–27°C, pH 8.0–9.0, hardness 10–20°H, native range (Lake Tanganyika), habitat (rocky littoral), diet, and trade-confusion note distinguishing S. petricola from S. lucipinnis. Confirmed rarity in trade and cuckoo-parasitism breeding strategy.

additional.name: FishBase — Synodontis petricola
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 14 cm TL), taxonomy (Mochokidae, Siluriformes), original description (Mathes 1959), native range (Lake Tanganyika basin: DRC, Tanzania, Zambia, Burundi), temperature tolerance, and pH range. Confirmed depth range and rocky habitat.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Synodontis Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Synodontis care overview; confirmed omnivorous sinking-food diet, group-keeping recommendation, nocturnal feeding habit, and copper sensitivity for scaleless catfish. Supplemented hobbyist guidance on tank size and Tanganyika water parameters.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Synodontis petricola species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed species-confusion history with S. lucipinnis, adult size ~14 cm for true S. petricola vs ~9 cm for S. lucipinnis, Tanganyikan hard-alkaline water requirements (pH 8.0–9.0), nocturnal feeding, group-keeping of 3+ individuals, and cuckoo parasitism breeding note.

additional.name: Wright, J.J. & Bailey, R.M. (2006) — Systematic revision distinguishing S. lucipinnis from S. petricola, via FishBase reference
additional.notes: Foundational taxonomic reference confirming formal separation of S. lucipinnis from S. petricola in 2006 based on morphometric and meristic characters. Establishes that most trade "petricola" specimens are S. lucipinnis, and that true S. petricola is the larger of the two species (up to 14 cm).

fish.breedingNotes: The Petricola Catfish is an egg-scatterer with reported cuckoo-parasitism of mouthbrooding cichlids in the wild, depositing eggs near a spawning pair so that fry are inadvertently taken up and incubated by the cichlid host. Captive breeding is uncommon; when it does occur, eggs are deposited in rock crevices and adults will consume them if not removed promptly. Fry require a separate rearing tank with appropriately fine foods and typically reach sexual maturity at around 18 to 24 months.
